Title: 'Just cutting ribbons': Tok Mat takes jab at PH's Negri track record
Article:
REMBAU: Negri Sembilan needs to regain the pace of development achieved under the Barisan Nasional administration, says former menteri besar Datuk Seri Mohamad Hasan.
The BN deputy chairman said state development had stalled after the coalition lost power in 2018.
"Negri Sembilan is close to Kuala Lumpur, the airport, and Port Klang, yet there is practically nothing to show for it.
"Two terms of work amounted to just cutting ribbons, with no real changes," he said, referring to the Pakatan Harapan administration.
He said this during a community gathering at Angsa Mas while campaigning for the state election.
Mohamad, who is commonly known as Tok Mat, said the state's residents deserved better.
"It is a loss for the people of Negeri Sembilan to have nothing," he said.
"Negri Sembilan's per capita income is ranked sixth, lower than Melaka. It should be higher, but it is not, because Negri Sembilan's economic growth over five years failed to match the national economic growth level," he said.
Mohamad added that BN had previously drawn up the 30-year Negeri Sembilan Development and Modernisation Plan 2015-2045 to boost the state economy.
"Unfortunately, we lost in 2018. Former Prime Minister Datuk Seri Mohd Najib Razak wanted us to launch it before the 2018 general election, but I asked to wait until after the polls.
"Regrettably, we failed to form the government again due to slander. BN must take over the administration of Negri Sembilan again," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ringgit opens higher against major currencies, softer vs US dollar
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The ringgit opened higher against most major currencies on Friday but eased against the US dollar as markets turned risk-off, with crude oil prices continuing to climb and the Trump administration stepping up its trade protectionist policies.
At 8.01am, the local currency was at 4.0925/0980 versus the greenback, compared with 4.0870/0910 at yesterday's close.
Bank Muamalat Malaysia Bhd chief economist Dr Mohd Afzanizam Abdul Rashid said United States President Donald Trump has announced additional import tariffs of between 10 per cent and 12.5 per cent on 60 economies, including Malaysia, citing forced labour practices as the reason.
"Trump remains adamant on using tariffs as a means to boost its economy after the major setback in February when the US Supreme Court annulled the previous tariff measures," he told Bernama.
He added that the local note is expected to trade cautiously today within the RM4.08 to RM4.10 range against the US dollar.
Meanwhile, the ringgit traded higher against a basket of major currencies at today's opening.
It strengthened against the Japanese yen to 2.4971/5006 from 2.5015/5041 at Thursday's close, appreciated vis-a-vis the euro to 4.6556/6619 from 4.6641/6686 yesterday, and went up versus the British pound to 5.4483/4557 from 5.4647/4701 previously.
The local currency rose against the Singapore dollar to 3.1651/1696 from 3.1667/1701 yesterday, but inched down versus the Indonesian rupiah to 228.1/228.5 from 227.8/228.1, and fell vis-a-vis the Thai baht to 12.0937/1153 from 12.0860/1021.
The ringgit was flat against the Philippine peso at 6.62/6.64 from 6.62/6.63.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Japan's factory activity stays firm as output surges in July, PMI shows
Article:
TOKYO: Japan's manufacturing activity remained broadly firm in July with output rising at its fastest rate since February 2014, while uncertainty about the Middle East situation continued to weigh on firms, a business survey showed on Friday.
The S&P Global flash Japan Manufacturing Purchasing Managers' Index (PMI) came in at 54.7 in July, slightly softer than 54.8 in June. A reading above 50.0 indicates expansion, while below that level signals contraction.
A key sub-index for factory output accelerated further, with the index climbing to its highest level since February 2014. Manufacturers also recorded the sharpest increase in new orders in just over five years, helping drive a moderate expansion in overall new business.
Service sector growth softened, with the flash services PMI easing to 51.9 from 52.2.
Total new export business expanded at the fastest pace in four months as overseas demand for goods improved, while foreign demand for services slipped again.
Employment across Japan's private sector rose for a 34th straight month, while backlogs of work accumulated at the strongest pace since February's series record.
Input cost inflation slowed to a three-month low, but output charges rose faster as services firms raised prices at the quickest pace in more than 12 years.
Usamah Bhatti, economics associate director at S&P Global Market Intelligence, said: "Underlying data showed diverging sector trends, with a stronger expansion in manufacturing contrasting with softer growth in services."
Firms remained optimistic about the year-ahead outlook, though confidence weakened from June amid uncertainty around the war in the Middle East, especially the impact on energy and raw material prices, as well as supply chain disruption.
"Manufacturers continued to report efforts to build stocks of goods and raw materials amid ongoing supply chain disruption and higher prices linked to the conflict, despite a slight easing in overall cost inflation over the month," Bhatti said.
The flash Japan composite PMI rose to 53.1 from 52.8, its highest since February and its 16th straight month above 50.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Lazaro: Myanmar returing to Asean official meetings 'still far off'
Article:
MANILA: The re-engagement of Myanmar leaders in Asean meetings is "still far off" and unlikely to happen within 2026 or during Singapore's chairmanship next year.
At a press conference following the 59th Asean Foreign Ministers' Meeting (AMM) in Pasay City on Thursday, Philippine Foreign Secretary Ma. Theresa Lazaro said there were still "questions" over the Myanmar government's compliance with the Five-Point Consensus (5PC), which remains the bloc's "primary reference" in addressing the crisis.
"I guess, right now I can say it's still far off. At this point in time, it's still the Five-Point Consensus that is the foundational document we are looking at," she said, according to the Philippine News Agency (PNA).
"During discussions with the foreign minister of Myanmar, there have been many questions on their compliance with the Five-Point Consensus. It may not happen yet, but let's see the compliance they can show," she said.
However, she said Asean is "patient" and will wait to see "whatever they can deliver" in terms of 5PC implementation.
"If there is compliance, then that means it will change. There is no view that a number of countries do not want Myanmar to take its seat. It is really about compliance with the 5PC," she said.
On humanitarian assistance, Lazaro confirmed she will lead a delegation to Myanmar in the fourth quarter of 2026, visiting areas occupied by ethnic organisations.
"The aim is to reach out to vulnerable communities in Myanmar.
"Of course, permits will have to be obtained from the junta government, and that will reflect their willingness to engage with us," she said.
The mission will be coordinated with the Asean Coordinating Centre for Humanitarian Assistance (AHA Centre) based in Jakarta, along with international humanitarian organisations including the United Nations Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs, Médecins Sans Frontières and the World Food Programme. — BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump to impose forced labour duties on Friday as temporary 10 per cent US tariffs expire
Article:
WASHINGTON: The Trump administration on Friday will impose new tariffs of 10 per cent and 12.5 per cent on goods from 60 trading partners, including the European Union, over allegations of lax enforcement of forced labour bans, just as a temporary 10 per cent global tariff expires.
The move is the White House's latest effort to restore President Donald Trump's campaign vision of a near-global tariff after the US Supreme Court in February struck down his "reciprocal" duties of 10 per cent to 50 per cent imposed last year under a national emergencies law to try to shrink the US trade deficit.
The new tariffs, announced on Thursday in a Federal Register notice, will cover 99.4 per cent of US imports but include numerous product exemptions, such as oil and gas, fertiliser and certain food items.
Imposed under Section 301 of the Trade Act of 1974, the new duties allow the administration to maintain a tariff floor on virtually all US imports despite the Supreme Court setback. The tariffs are also likely to face less legal risk than those struck down in February, as Section 301 has survived prior court challenges.
Trump responded to the February Supreme Court ruling by imposing a temporary 10 per cent tariff for 150 days that expires at 12:01am EDT on Friday (0401 GMT). The new duties will take effect at that exact same moment, with goods in transit exempted until 12:01am EDT on July 28.
"The United States has had a forced labour import ban for nearly a century, and rigorously enforces it. It's well past time for our trading partners to do the same," US Trade Representative Jamieson Greer said in a statement.
"Today's action will begin to correct what is both a human rights abuse and distortive trade practice to improve the welfare of workers everywhere."
Greer has previously pledged that for countries that have reached trade deals with Washington that have capped US tariff rates, the new forced labour duties would not push them above those caps. The deals include anti-forced labour provisions.
Under the final determination, the US will impose a 10 per cent duty on goods from Argentina, Bangladesh, Britain, Cambodia, Canada, Ecuador, El Salvador, Guatemala, Honduras, India, Indonesia, Jordan, Malaysia, Mexico, Pakistan, Sri Lanka, Trinidad and Tobago.
The European Union, Taiwan, Japan, South Korea and Switzerland were assigned rates that, combined with pre-existing most-favoured-nation tariff rates, would total 10 per cent or 12.5 per cent. The other 38 countries, including China, were assigned a 12.5 per cent rate.
SIMILAR RATES, DIFFERENT TARIFF
"As expected, the forced labour tariffs largely replicate current tariff levels as negotiated in various reciprocal trade agreements, and replace the 10 per cent tariffs under Section 122 that expire on Friday," said Tim Brightbill, a trade law partner with Wiley Rein in Washington.
A senior Trump administration official disputed suggestions that the forced labour tariffs were simply a direct replacement for the expiring levies despite the timing, similar duty rates and vast coverage of nearly all US imports.
The official said the US has stronger import bans on goods made with forced labour and enforces them more rigidly than any other country, giving rivals an unfair trading advantage over the US.
Both Democrats and Republicans in Congress have been calling for the eradication of forced labour from global supply chains, "so we're really responding to that call", the official said.
The official added that Trump would "always use the tools at his disposal to achieve his trade policy objectives, and that includes tariffs".
Many goods will be exempted from the duties, including oil and gas, fertiliser, certain foodstuffs and goods that are already subject to Section 232 national security tariffs, such as autos, steel, aluminium and copper, the official said.
Other goods that comply with the US-Mexico-Canada Agreement on trade will also be exempted because of the highly integrated North American supply chain and high levels of US content in the goods.
The final Section 301 unfair trade practices tariffs largely track the forced labour duties proposed on June 1.
Goods from countries that have passed adequate anti-forced labour laws will be levied at the lower 10 per cent rate, and imports from those with inadequate bans will be subject to the higher 12.5 per cent rate.
Recent actions and legislation by some countries, including India, moved them to the 10 per cent tariff rate since the duties were first proposed.
After public hearings on the forced labour duties, the US Trade Representative's office also added some new exemptions, including for pig iron, certain sugar products, animal and seed products and certain chemicals.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US Forces launch 13th consecutive night of strikes on Iranian military targets
Article:
TEHERAN: United States (US) forces launched strikes against Iranian military targets, marking the 13th consecutive night of operations, US Central Command (CENTCOM) announced Thursday, Anadolu Ajansi (AA) reported.
CENTCOM said the latest strikes began at 6.45pm ET (2245GMT).
"This is the 13th consecutive night of strikes aimed to hold Iran accountable and diminish threats from the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps to commercial shipping," the command said on the US social media platform X. — BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Wall St falls as tech earnings spark AI spending worries and oil hits US$100
Article:
NEW YORK: Wall Street indexes closed lower on Thursday with Nasdaq sinking more than two per cent as the latest earnings updates from large technology companies revived concerns about heavy AI spending, while soaring oil prices amped up inflation worries, lifting bond yields.
The S&P 500 fell more than one per cent as losses were broad-based after investors were unimpressed by second-quarter results from Alphabet and Tesla, the first of the heavyweight "Magnificent Seven" companies to report results this season.
Stocks came under additional pressure as Brent crude oil futures settled above USUS$100 a barrelfor the first time since May, and US oil futures settled above US$92. Intensifying Middle East hostilities fanned worries about global oil supplies.
The US military launched another round of air strikes on Iran, and Iran fired at US bases in nearby countries. US President Donald Trump vowed "major military punishment" for Iran and Houthis, after the Yemeni fighters struck two Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea.
The resulting surge in oil prices prompted worries about inflation days before the next Federal Reserve policy meeting.
"Oil prices rising at this clip pose a meaningful macro and market risk," said Matt Miskin, co-chief investment strategist at Manulife John Hancock Investments. He added that low unemployment data would pressure the Fed to focus on fighting inflation.
He also pointed to the lack of enthusiasm, even for strong earnings reports as investors struggle to justify high valuations.
"The numbers in aggregate are great but many companies have had a story that's caused the stock to fall," said Miskin, pointing to worries about capital spending as one example.
"Any chink in the armor and the stocks are being sold off."
The Dow Jones Industrial Average fell 506.93 points, or 0.97 per cent, to 51,711.65, the S&P 500 lost 90.66 points, or 1.21 per cent, to 7,408.30 and the Nasdaq Composite lost 553.21 points, or 2.15 per cent, to 25,137.69.
The S&P 500's 11 major industry sectors were a mixed bag with just four advancing on the day.
The biggest gainer was industrials, up 1.77 per cent with boosts from defense stocks including Lockheed Martin and RTX.
Shares in Lockheed rallied 10.5 per cent after it lifted 2026 sales and profit forecasts.
RTX was the S&P 500's second biggest boost as it finished up 7.3 per cent after it raised its 2026 sales and profit forecasts on demand for commercial aircraft maintenance and military systems.
Shares of Google's parent, Alphabet, sank seven per cent, making it the second-biggest drag on the benchmark index after it reported higher spending plans while it also burned cash.
The stock's fall also dragged down the communication services index 5.20 per cent, making it the weakest of the 11 sectors.
Consumer discretionary was the second-biggest loser, ending down 5.12 per cent. Its biggest drag was from Tesla, whose shares tumbled 14.5 per cent, after it reported negative free cash flow in the second quarter for the first time in more than two years.
Wall Street's fear gauge, the CBOE Volatility Index finished up 2.06 points at 18.7, after earlier hitting 20.3, which was its highest level in almost a month.
With oil prices rising, inflation worries pushed US 10-year yields to their highest levels since early 2025.
However traders were still betting on a roughly 64 per cent probability that the Fed would keep interest rates unchanged next week, according to CME Group's FedWatch tool.
The volatile Philadelphia semiconductor index ended down 0.5 per cent as investors anticipated results from chip giant Intel.
Shares in analog chip company Texas Instruments finished down 3 per cent despite forecasting quarterly revenue above estimates.
However, shares in Thermo Fisher Scientific rallied 8.7 per cent after the medical equipment maker raised its annual profit forecast and beat estimates for second-quarter results.
Declining issues outnumbered advancers by a 2.99-to-1 ratio on the NYSE, where there were 82 new highs and 354 new lows.
On the Nasdaq, 1,374 stocks rose and 3,467 fell as declining issues outnumbered advancers by a 2.52-to-1 ratio.
The S&P 500 posted 17 new 52-week highs and 15 new lows while the Nasdaq Composite recorded 41 new highs and 184 new lows.
On US exchanges, 16.21 billion shares changed hands compared with the 18.51 billion average for the last 20 sessions.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Asean, China making 'good progress' on COC talks
Article:
MANILA: Asean and China are making "good progress" on the negotiations of a code of conduct (COC) in the South China Sea, said Philippine Foreign Secretary Ma. Theresa Lazaro.
Lazaro said all parties still intend to finish the code "within the year" against the backdrop of recent developments in the South China Sea, Philippine news agency (PNA) reported.
"Asean now and China (are) geared towards the conclusion - hopefully the conclusion - of the code of conduct by the end of this year.
"And this seems to be the focus of many of the countries, and I can see some political will from all the countries involved," she said in a press conference in Parañaque City on Thursday.
Negotiators are hastening talks through monthly in-person meetings to resolve four key "milestone issues" in the COC draft.
These include establishing the connection between the COC and the non-binding Asean Declaration of Conduct (DOC) on the South China Sea; determining whether the final document is legally binding or not; the geographical scope; and the definition of terms, such as self-restraint.
"If we see it from our point of view as the progress being made, the terms of reference is very important - let's say (the definition of) self-restraint, now, there's been some kind of consensus, a growing consensus, and that's, for us, good progress," Lazaro said.
The foreign ministers of Asean, China, and several major powers, including the United States, Australia, and Japan met in Manila for the 59th Asean Foreign Ministers' Meeting and Related Meetings. – Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Hiring right: Putting the right people in the right jobs
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Hiring the right people is one of the most important responsibilities of any leader. Every successful organisation is built upon individuals who possess the right skills, the right attitude, and the right values. While strategies, technology, and systems can all contribute to success, it is people who ultimately determine whether an organisation thrives or struggles. A poor hiring decision can be costly, disruptive, and time-consuming, while the right appointment can transform performance, strengthen culture, and inspire others to excel.
Many organisations focus heavily on qualifications and experience during recruitment. Whilst these are certainly important, they should never be the only criteria. Technical knowledge can often be taught, refined, or updated through training. Character, integrity, resilience, and a willingness to learn are far more difficult to develop. Hiring should therefore assess both competence and character, ensuring that candidates possess the personal qualities needed to succeed alongside the technical ability required for the role.
Before recruiting anyone, leaders must first understand exactly what they are looking for. A vague job description often leads to vague hiring decisions. Every role should have clearly defined responsibilities, expectations, and measures of success. When leaders know precisely what outcomes are expected, they are far more likely to identify candidates whose strengths genuinely match the position rather than simply selecting someone who interviews well.
Recruitment should never become a race to fill an empty position quickly. Vacancies often create pressure, but making a rushed decision usually creates even bigger problems later. Taking additional time to identify the right candidate is almost always a better investment than appointing the wrong person simply to solve an immediate staffing issue. Patience during recruitment frequently saves months of frustration afterwards.
A successful recruitment process also requires consistency. Every applicant should be assessed using the same criteria and asked similar questions that explore both technical capability and behavioural qualities. Structured interviews help reduce bias and encourage fair comparisons between candidates. They also provide stronger evidence for making informed decisions rather than relying on instinct or first impressions alone.
One of the most valuable qualities to look for is adaptability. The modern workplace changes constantly, requiring individuals who can learn new skills, solve unfamiliar problems, and remain positive during uncertainty. Employees who demonstrate curiosity and a commitment to continuous improvement often outperform those who rely solely on past experience. A willingness to grow can be more valuable than an impressive history of previous achievements.
Communication skills also deserve careful attention. Regardless of the role, every employee interacts with colleagues, customers, suppliers, or stakeholders in some capacity. Individuals who communicate clearly, listen actively, and work collaboratively strengthen the entire organisation. Strong communication reduces misunderstandings, improves teamwork, and creates an environment where problems are identified and resolved more effectively.
Equally important is cultural alignment. This does not mean hiring people who all think alike. Diversity of thought and experience drives innovation and better decision-making. Instead, cultural alignment means ensuring that candidates share the organisation's core values, demonstrate respect for others, and contribute positively to the working environment. Skills may secure employment, but values often determine long-term success.
Reference checks should never be treated as a routine administrative exercise. Speaking with previous employers can provide valuable insights into a candidate's reliability, professionalism, work ethic, and ability to collaborate with others. References often confirm strengths highlighted during interviews whilst also revealing potential concerns that may not otherwise become apparent.
Hiring decisions should rarely be made by one individual alone. Including several interviewers provides different perspectives and reduces the influence of personal bias. One interviewer may notice technical competence, another may recognise leadership potential, whilst another may identify communication strengths or concerns. Collective judgement often produces more balanced and accurate recruitment decisions.
It is equally important to remember that recruitment is a two-way process. Candidates are also evaluating the organisation. Honest conversations about expectations, challenges, opportunities, and workplace culture help ensure both parties make informed decisions. Overselling a position may secure acceptance initially, but disappointment often follows if reality fails to match expectations.
Even after selecting the right candidate, the recruitment process is not complete. Effective onboarding plays a crucial role in helping new employees become productive and engaged. Clear guidance, structured induction, regular feedback, and supportive leadership all increase confidence during the early months of employment. Many recruitment failures are not caused by poor hiring decisions but by inadequate support after appointment.
Leaders should also evaluate the effectiveness of their recruitment decisions over time. Reviewing employee performance, retention rates, and overall contribution provides valuable lessons for future hiring. Continuous improvement should apply to recruitment just as much as it applies to every other organisational process. Learning from both successful and unsuccessful appointments helps strengthen future decisions.
Perhaps the greatest mistake organisations make is hiring purely to fill today's gap rather than considering tomorrow's needs. The strongest candidates are those who not only perform well in their current role but also possess the potential to grow as responsibilities evolve. Recruiting for future capability creates stronger succession planning and builds resilience throughout the organisation.
Ultimately, hiring the right people for the right jobs is about matching capability with opportunity. It requires preparation, patience, consistency, careful evaluation, and sound judgement. Organisations that invest time in selecting individuals whose skills, values, and potential align with their objectives build stronger teams, healthier workplace cultures, and more sustainable success. Every recruitment decision shapes the future, making the pursuit of the right person for the right role one of the wisest investments any leader can make.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US pharmaceutical tariffs could reshape global supply chains
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The United States' proposed tariffs of up to 200 per cent on imported generic medicines may reverberate far beyond its market, with industry experts warning of a major reshaping of global pharmaceutical supply chains, manufacturing strategies and investment flows.
While Malaysia has limited direct exposure to pharmaceutical exports to the US, the country could still feel the knock-on effects as manufacturers reassess production locations, supply networks and sourcing strategies.
This will potentially affect active pharmaceutical ingredient (API) supplies, production costs and medicine availability, they added.
The proposed policy includes a two-year zero-tariff transition period before tariffs of 100 per cent and later 200 per cent are imposed, with the objective of encouraging pharmaceutical manufacturers to relocate production to the US.
No Immediate Concern For Patients
Association of Private Hospitals Malaysia president Datuk Dr Kuljit Singh said the overall impact would depend on how the policy is ultimately implemented and how the global pharmaceutical industry responds.
He noted that trade measures and geopolitical conflicts inevitably disrupt supply chains and contribute to higher business costs.
"For now, however, there is no immediate cause for concern for patients receiving care at private hospitals.
"We will continue to monitor developments closely and work with our suppliers and partners to ensure the uninterrupted supply of essential medicines and the continued delivery of quality healthcare services," he told Business Times.
Long-term Risks Outweigh Short-term Opportunities
Galen Centre chief executive officer Azrul Mohd Khalib believes Malaysian pharmaceutical exporters with significant exposure to the US market could face considerable commercial challenges once the tariffs are fully implemented.
Although the US accounts for around 15 per cent of Malaysia's pharmaceutical exports, Azrul said the impact could be severe for companies dependent on that market.
"The indirect effects could be more significant, particularly if global manufacturers redirect products, investment and production capacity to other markets.
"This could intensify competition for Malaysian companies and disrupt established supply chains," he said.
Azrul also warned that Malaysia may eventually experience higher medicine prices if global pharmaceutical companies prioritise more profitable markets or if production costs continue rising.
"We are already seeing this happening due to the armed conflict around the Strait of Hormuz.
"Malaysia must strengthen local manufacturing and ensure medicine procurement considers supply security and resilience, rather than focusing only on the lowest price," he said.
Possible Disruption, Indirectly
Malaysian Medical Association (MMA) president Datuk Dr Thirunavukarasu Rajoo said it was still too early to determine the extent of any impact on medicine prices or access in Malaysia.
However, Thirunavukarasu said the global pharmaceutical supply chain is highly interconnected and any increase in manufacturing costs or disruption in the supply of APIs could create cost pressures across the supply chain.
"The actual impact on Malaysia will depend on how global manufacturers respond and how global supply chains evolve.
"The situation should therefore be monitored closely to ensure patients continue to have timely access to essential medicines," he said.
Thirunavukarasu said there is potential for indirect disruption if manufacturers reprioritise production, adjust supply chains or face higher production costs.
"While there is currently no evidence of widespread medicine shortages in Malaysia arising from these tariffs, it is important that any emerging supply issues are identified early before they affect patient care," he said.
Patients Remain Priority
Thirunavukarasu welcomed the Health Ministry's efforts to strengthen pharmaceutical supply chain resilience, including continuous monitoring of global developments and engagement with industry stakeholders.
He said proactive collaboration between regulators, healthcare professionals and manufacturers would become increasingly important should global pharmaceutical trade become more fragmented.
"Ultimately, while trade policies are shaped by economic and geopolitical considerations, they should never compromise patients' access to safe, effective and affordable medicines.
"Our shared priority must remain uninterrupted access to essential medicines for all Malaysians," he said.
No Immediate Direct Impact
Pharmaniaga managing director Datuk Zulkifli Jafar said the company does not currently export pharmaceutical products to the US and therefore expects no direct impact from the new tariff regime.
However, Zulkifli acknowledged that the global pharmaceutical supply chain remains highly interconnected and indirect implications could arise if suppliers or vendors are affected by the tariffs.
"We will continue to monitor these developments closely and engage with our suppliers to assess any potential impact on cost, availability and continuity of supply.
"Where necessary, we will review our sourcing strategies, identify alternative suppliers and maintain appropriate inventory levels for critical materials to strengthen supply resilience," he said.
He added that Pharmaniaga would continue investing in its pharmaceutical and biopharmaceutical manufacturing capabilities to support Malaysia's long-term medicine security and strengthen the resilience of the national healthcare system.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump reports over US$1.4 billion in income from crypto ventures
Article:
TORONTO/WASHINGTON: US President Donald Trump reported more than US$1.4 billion in income from his family's crypto ventures last year, showing how Trump now derives most of his income from digital assets that have benefited from his policies, according to a review of his latest financial disclosures on Tuesday.
The filings, his annual disclosure for 2025 with the US Office of Government Ethics, showed his companies received almost US$800 million from World Liberty Financial, a crypto venture he and his sons co-founded.
That income, which the president splits with family members, included more than US$520 million from sales of crypto tokens and more than US$250 million from the sale of interests in the World Liberty business.
Trump reported another US$635 million from the sale of his Trump meme coins. The news underlines how crypto has transformed the president's fortunes.
In his disclosure a year ago, for example, the president reported US$57.35 million from token sales at World Liberty, which then leapt nine-fold in this year's filing.
Reuters recently estimated the Trump family has made at least US$2.3 billion from crypto-related projects since Trump returned to the White House in 2025.
On taking office, Trump began to put in place policies and initiatives that the industry saw as beneficial, from implementing federal rules for stablecoins to dialling back policing of the industry by the US Justice Department and the Securities and Exchange Commission.
For 2025, the president also reported more than US$80 million in income from settlements with various media companies and US$52 million in income from his company licensing his name to overseas property developers, driven principally by deals with Middle Eastern partners.
White House spokesperson Anna Kelly said in a statement: "Neither the President nor his family has ever engaged — or will ever engage — in conflicts of interest. President Trump proudly made the United States the crypto capital of the world through executive actions."
Kelly added: "All actions by President Trump and his administration are taken in the best interest of the American people — and any so-called 'reporters' pushing otherwise are recycling the same, tired, false narrative that Democrats and the legacy media have been pushing for a decade."
While the White House has previously said the president's business interests are currently overseen by his children, the president remains the beneficiary of the assets in the trust that ultimately receives the income.
NEW WEALTH DRIVEN BY CRYPTO
Although crypto is by far the largest driver of income for Trump, his traditional businesses — in particular golf courses and resorts — continued to bring in millions.
Trump reported a 15 per cent rise in revenue at his golf and resort facilities to just over US$500 million in 2025. The strongest increases were at clubs where the president has spent considerable time since his 2025 inauguration.
Revenue at his Mar-a-Lago club in Florida, which Trump dubbed the Winter White House, soared to US$77 million from US$50 million in 2024, while revenue at his golf club in nearby West Palm Beach jumped 27 per cent. Revenue fell at Trump's Los Angeles course last year.
Trump also secured a more than US$50 million loan last year from Charles Schwab Bank, the filings showed. They did not say what the loan was for.
Trump hosted winners of his second annual meme coin contest at Mar-a-Lago in April.
Trump's income from his real estate interests — the business in which he made his name — had less spectacular growth. He reported income from a dozen significant commercial real estate ventures, mainly interests in buildings he built or acquired decades ago.
The filing does not give specific rent figures for properties such as Trump Tower in New York but rather income ranges. For most, the income range in 2025 was the same or lower than Trump reported a decade prior.
A spokesperson for the Trump family business, The Trump Organisation, said in a statement that "the breadth and depth of this filing further underscores our commitment to transparency.
At nearly 1,000 pages, it represents one of the most comprehensive financial disclosure reports ever submitted and demonstrates a level of financial transparency unmatched in presidential history."
A spokesperson for World Liberty Financial declined to comment.
Don Fox, a former acting head of the federal ethics office, which oversees ethics regulations for federal workers and reviews financial disclosures, including Trump's, said presidents and vice presidents are exempted from the ethics laws that prohibit conflicts of interest among executive branch employees.
"Every president in the post-Watergate era has managed his finances as though he were subject to conflicts of interest," said Fox. "With Trump, those norms are just totally out the window."
"He makes the case better than anyone that it's time for additional ethics reforms. I think in terms of legislation, one thing that could be done would be to limit the types of investments he and the vice president ... can hold."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US' Section 301 not an indictment of Malaysia's practicing forced labour: MICCI [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The United States' Section 301 measures should not be seen as an indication that Malaysia practices forced labour, but rather reflect Washington's tightening regulatory expectations on supply chain governance and import controls, a top industry executive said.
Malaysian International Chamber of Commerce and Industry (MICCI) president Christina Tee said the proposed measures underscore the need for Malaysian exporters to strengthen supply chain transparency, traceability and due diligence as global trade requirements become increasingly stringent.
While the proposed measures could increase compliance costs and create greater uncertainty for exporters serving the US market, Tee said businesses should view them as part of the evolving global trade landscape rather than a Malaysia-specific issue.
"Pending the US' final determination on the new tariffs, businesses are mindful that any new trade measures could increase compliance requirements, operating costs and uncertainty for exporters serving the US market," Tee told Business Times.
The Trump administration is preparing new tariffs targeting 60 trading partners including Malaysia over alleged failures to act against forced labour, with analysts expecting the duties to range between 10 per cent and 12.5 per cent.
The proposed tariffs are expected to replace the temporary 10 per cent global levy imposed earlier this year, which is due to expire on Friday.
Tee said local exporters have demonstrated resilience amid an increasingly uncertain global trading environment and evolving geopolitical developments since last year.
She said many exporters have proactively reviewed sourcing strategies, diversified raw material procurement where feasible, and strengthened supplier due diligence and supply chain traceability to meet rising customer and regulatory expectations.
However, she noted that while such practices are already embedded among many multinational corporations and larger exporters, small and medium enterprises (SMEs) may continue to face greater challenges in meeting increasingly complex compliance requirements.
Tee said continued constructive engagement between Malaysia and the US would be important in achieving a fair outcome that supports trade, investment and business confidence.
The chamber also urged businesses to continue strengthening compliance frameworks and supply chain resilience to remain competitive in an increasingly regulated global marketplace.
"MICCI believes any trade measures should be transparent, predictable and evidence-based, while recognising the continued efforts by Malaysian businesses and the government to strengthen labour standards and supply chain governance," she added.
Federation of Malaysian Manufacturing (FMM) president Jacob Lee Chor Kok said labour-intensive export sectors, including rubber gloves, medical consumables, textiles and apparel, as well as specialised industrial manufacturing, would face the most immediate impact if the proposed tariffs are implemented.
Lee said many Malaysian manufacturers exporting to the US already operate under strict customer-driven labour compliance requirements, including regular audits, supplier codes of conduct and supply chain traceability obligations.
"While high-volume sectors face compliance scrutiny, the broader concern is that a blanket tariff penalises compliant manufacturers and disrupts established, long-standing trade and supply-chain relationships between Malaysia and the US," Lee said.
He added that FMM has urged the US Trade Representative (USTR) to retain existing Annex A exclusions, particularly for electrical and electronics (E&E) products, semiconductors and related lines, which are vital to global technology supply chains.
Lee said additional tariffs could also increase costs for US importers, manufacturers and consumers, affecting pricing, product availability and lead times.
Despite the potential challenges, Malaysia could benefit from supply chain realignment as multinational corporations seek alternative manufacturing locations under strategies such as China+1 and regional diversification.
"As multinational corporations review their exposure under strategies like China+1 or regional diversification, countries with a lower tariff tier, such as 10 per cent versus 12.5 per cent imposed on non-compliant nations, and transparent labour frameworks become far more attractive destination hubs.
"However, capturing these redirected trade flows requires proactive efforts to showcase our robust labour governance, ensuring that incoming investment sees Malaysia as a low-risk, ethical manufacturing hub," Lee said.
Lee added that the country's mature semiconductor ecosystem, strong manufacturing capabilities and improving labour governance framework could position the country as an attractive destination for companies seeking reliable and compliant supply chains.
He identified E&E and semiconductors, machinery and precision engineering, medical devices, and green technology components such as solar and electric vehicle-related products as sectors that could benefit from redirected investments.
"Malaysia can indeed benefit from supply chain realignment, provided we continue demonstrating high labour compliance standards relative to regional peers," Lee said.
He added that countries with transparent labour frameworks and lower tariff exposure would become more attractive destinations for foreign direct investment as companies review supply chain risks.
Investment, Trade and Industry Minister Datuk Seri Johari Abdul Ghani on Wednesday said Malaysia and the US had discussed ways to strengthen bilateral trade ties, including ongoing Section 301 and Section 232 investigations that could affect Malaysian exports.
Johari hopes any trade measures introduced by the US would take into account the shared interests of both countries.
"Such measures should complement their respective economies and support stable and resilient global supply chains," he said in a Facebook post following his working visit to Washington.
He said both countries agreed to continue resolving trade-related issues through constructive dialogue, closer engagement and transparent information sharing.
"These efforts are important to safeguard Malaysia's trade and investment interests while reinforcing our position as a stable, trusted and competitive economic partner," Johari said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Selangor Wholesale Market fire: Firefighters faced risk of explosions from 2,000 methane gas cylinders [WATCH]
Article:
SERDANG: Firefighters faced high risks while battling a fire at Selangor wholesale market here after about 2,000 cylinders of methane gas were found inside the burning premises.
Selangor Fire and Rescue Department assistant director of operations Ashrul Riezal Asbar said several explosions occurred during the early stages of the operation, but the situation was brought under control.
"Based on our assessment at the scene, we were informed that there were about 2,000 cylinders of methane gas inside the premises.
"Several explosions did occur initially, but we managed to control the situation.
"At present, firefighters are still carrying out extinguishing operations at three storage areas at the back of the premises," he said during a press conference at the scene.
He said the department received an emergency call at 11.04pm, with the first fire engine from the Seri Kembangan Fire and Rescue Station arriving nine minutes later.
The fire was brought under control at 1.20am, and no casualties have been reported so far.
"The operation involves 68 personnel from 15 fire stations in Selangor, Kuala Lumpur and Putrajaya, assisted by 11 voluntary firefighting teams and police personnel," he said.
Ashrul said the operation was also hampered after five nearby fire hydrants could not be used as they were under the management of private parties.
"As a result, we relied fully on water supplies from fire engines and water tankers throughout the operation," he said.
He said the cause of the fire was still under investigation as firefighting operations had yet to be completed.
Earlier, Harian Metro reported that a massive fire destroyed a metal goods premises within the Selangor wholesale market area in Seri Kembangan, Selangor.
The blaze also spread to several nearby premises but was prevented from reaching the market building.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Retention ponds remain key defences against floods, says DID [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: While flood retention ponds across Kuala Lumpur and Selangor continue to play a crucial role in mitigating urban flood risks, extreme weather and rapid development mean they cannot be treated as a standalone solution.
Currently, Selangor is supported by more than 190 flood retention ponds in flood-prone areas, while Kuala Lumpur relies on 14 retention ponds and four major diversion structures integrated into the Kuala Lumpur Flood Mitigation (KLFM) framework, which includes the Smart Tunnel.
Drainage and Irrigation Department (DID) director-general Datuk Mohamad Radzi Abdul Talib said major retention ponds under DID's supervision remained fully operational, serving as vital buffers to reduce excess stormwater flowing into rivers and public drainage systems.
"During periods of heavy or prolonged rainfall, water levels in some ponds may rise significantly as they perform their intended function of temporarily storing excess rainwater," he told the
New Straits Times
.
"However, these facilities remain an important component of the  flood mitigation system by reducing peak flow rates, thereby helping to lower flood risks."
On  upgrading existing ponds into "smart" or multi-functional systems, Radzi said DID's  priority remained operational reliability.
"DID's priority is to ensure that flood retention ponds continue to function effectively."
However, he said DID would consider smart monitoring tools or multi-functional concepts for selected new or upgraded projects where technically feasible and supported by available funding.
On whether existing facilities were sufficient for future extreme weather events, Radzi said it depended on  rainfall intensity, climate change and broader urban development.
He said in dense urban centres like Kuala Lumpur, land constraints made building large-scale retention ponds almost
impossible.
"In Kuala Lumpur, land availability is extremely limited due to high levels of development. There is very limited opportunity to construct new large-scale flood retention ponds in the city."
He added that the KLFM system was designed to withstand up to a 100-year average recurrence interval  flood event, protecting against severe, extreme inundations well beyond normal seasonal downpours.
Due to limited space, he said DID's strategy relied   on non-structural measures and physical infrastructure.
Radzi said these included technical guidelines under the Urban Stormwater Management Manual for Malaysia, the Stormwater Management and Drainage Master Plan Study,  as well as specialised Flood Mitigation Master Plans.
To prevent surrounding development from overburdening municipal drainage, he said DID enforced strict stormwater management rules on private projects.
He said in Selangor, property developments exceeding 10.12ha were required to construct on-site flood retention ponds, while smaller developments must install on-site detention facilities to slow down stormwater runoff.
"For flood retention ponds serving a public mitigation
function, protection measures include land reservation, gazettement by authorities and strict development controls in surrounding areas to safeguard their operational capacity.
"Once completed, developer-built retention ponds are typically handed over to local authorities for long-term protection and management."
Radzi said maintaining the critical assets came  with operational challenges,  requiring DID to regularly conduct  inspections, desilting, sediment removal, vegetation control and electrical repair works.
He said land clearing and upstream earthworks deposited heavy sediment that gradually reduced pond storage capacity, while rapid building construction and road development accelerated stormwater runoff entering drainage networks.
DID also contended with land ownership disputes and encroachment issues that restricted  access for maintenance, where these operational hurdles were further compounded by ageing infrastructure that required periodic rehabilitation amid funding constraints.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US unveils new tariffs on 60 partners as Trump rebuilds trade agenda
Article:
WASHINGTON: The United States said on Thursday that it would impose new tariffs on 60 trading partners over forced labour concerns, replacing an expiring global duty rolled out by President Donald Trump earlier this year.
The levies, which take effect on Friday, range from 10 per cent to 12.5 per cent and impact major economies such as China, India and the European Union.
"The United States has had a forced labour import ban for nearly a century, and rigorously enforces it; it's well past time for our trading partners to do the same," said US Trade Representative Jamieson Greer in unveiling the duties.
The Trump administration has moved swiftly to rebuild the president's tariff wall after the Supreme Court struck down a host of his duties in February, dealing a blow to his ability to unleash steep levies at will.
After the legal setback, Trump tapped different authorities to reimpose a 10 per cent tariff on imports. But this only lasts 150 days, expiring on Friday.
The volley of new duties, initially proposed in June, will now take its place.
The measures were proposed after a months-long investigation and are considered more resistant to legal challenges than earlier moves.
Under Thursday's announcement, economies that have implemented a forced labour prohibition are hit with the lower 10 per cent rate. They include Canada, the EU and the United Kingdom.
Others were deemed to deserve harsher levies, receiving the higher 12.5 per cent tariff, a US official told reporters. Trading partners such as China and Japan are covered in this group.
Goods already facing Trump's sector-specific tariffs — like steel and aluminium — will not be impacted.
Goods entering under the US-Mexico-Canada free trade pact will also be exempt, a US official told reporters.
MAINTAINING LEVERAGE
Washington is separately investigating 16 economies over excess industrial capacity, in probes that could lead to additional duties.
These could result in different rates among countries eventually, as Trump had done before his legal setback.
The Trump administration's move to impose a baseline tariff while sustaining the threat of further duties ahead maintains leverage over its trading partners, trade lawyer Greta Peisch told AFP.
It also creates an incentive for countries to comply with trade pacts that they earlier struck, she added.
In spending time on investigations, officials want their incoming tariffs to be robust if there are court challenges, said Peisch, a former USTR general counsel who is now a partner at Wiley Rein.
"This makes it much more likely that they stay for the duration of Trump's term," signalling a "much more protectionist world's largest economy" moving forward, Josh Lipsky of the Atlantic Council think tank told AFP.
The Trump administration has been hunting for options that would allow it to aggressively deploy tariffs, said former US trade official Ryan Majerus.
In the longer term, Section 301 of the Trade Act of 1974, which Greer tapped to impose the latest duties, provides "more flexibility than people realise", Majerus said.
Once they are in place, officials can modify them based on new developments, added Majerus, a partner at King & Spalding.
'FRAGILE' DEALS
The latest salvo comes shortly after a separate 25 per cent tariff took effect on various Brazilian goods, with Washington accusing the Latin American giant of unfair trade practices after a year-long investigation.
This week, Trump also ordered new 50 per cent tariffs on many Canadian products, citing Ottawa's "discriminatory treatment" against American alcohol, automobile and dairy products.
The Canadian tariffs taking effect in a month relied on an untested legal provision, showing that Trump has "other tools in the toolkit" to wield, said Lipsky.
This signals that US tariff deals "are still fragile".
Nonetheless, the EU, which earlier signed a trade pact with the United States, expects Washington "will honour the commitments that are spelled out under the EU-US Joint Statement."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Justice Dept withdraws NY Times subpoenas over Trump plane report
Article:
NEW YORK: The US Justice Department on Thursday withdrew subpoenas issued to New York Times reporters over a story they wrote about security concerns with a plane donated to President Donald Trump by Qatar.
The Times said federal prosecutors agreed at a court hearing in New York before District Judge Arun Subramanian to "unilaterally withdraw the subpoenas at this time."
The newspaper had challenged the government requests for phone records of reporters and grand jury testimony as an attempt to "intimidate" the press and filed a motion to have them quashed.
Lawyers for the Times said "the government's actions violate the most basic First Amendment protections for newsgathering activity."
"They represent the latest salvo in an escalating series of attacks on journalists to intimidate them from engaging in reporting that the Trump administration openly detests," they said.
The subpoenas were issued after a Times report that the Qatari-gifted Air Force One lacked certain security countermeasures, including anti-missile defences.
Qatar's royal family donated the luxury airliner last year after Trump complained about the state of the two ageing jets that have served as the official US presidential planes since 1990.
Trump took his first flight on the retrofitted Boeing 747-8 plane on July 1, but questions swirled after he took an older jet home from a NATO meeting in Ankara on July 10.
Turkiye borders Iran, and hostilities between Washington and Teheran had flared up again at the time.
Trump denied any security concerns and said he was sending the new plane to a British airbase so it could be toured by US troops.
He later said the Qatari-gifted Air Force One needed to be sent off for a month to be "maxed out". He did not give details about how the plane would be "maxed out".
Critics have raised a host of ethical, constitutional and security concerns about the gifting of an aircraft worth hundreds of millions of dollars by a foreign power like Qatar.
The plane features a new Trump-designed livery in red, white and navy blue, replacing the previous white and light blue.
Two brand-new Boeing Air Force Ones are due to be delivered later this decade after a series of delays and cost overruns.
Reporters Without Borders (RSF) welcomed the withdrawal of the subpoenas.
"Today's outcome is a victory for The New York Times and for every journalist who relies on confidential sources to report in the public interest," RSF said. "The court has reaffirmed that the government cannot use its legal powers to intimidate reporters or erode the independence of the press."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump stuns Saudis by pinning nuclear deal to Israel ties
Article:
WASHINGTON: US President Donald Trump said on Thursday that a nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia depends on the kingdom recognising Israel, throwing the agreement into turmoil just a day after it was signed.
The apparently new condition comes despite Riyadh having long resisted joining the so-called Abraham Accords with Israel, insisting on a pathway to a Palestinian state.
It was not mentioned in the civilian nuclear deal announced on Wednesday, which had appeared to be a major win for the Saudis even as Trump wages war on Iran over its nuclear programme.
But Trump stunned the Saudis with his post on his Truth Social network, saying the deal "will be approved, but is totally subject to Saudi Arabia joining the very respected and successful Abraham Accords."
The White House insisted that Trump had previously told Saudi Arabia the deal was contingent on recognising Israel.
"The president is always the final dealmaker. He has said if they don't join the Abraham Accords, the deal is off," Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt told reporters.
"The president has spoken directly with the Saudis about this in numerous conversations in the past, and the administration will continue to engage in those conversations."
HISTORIC LEAP
Israel's Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said recognition by Saudi Arabia, a regional power and home to Islam's holiest sites, would mark a "historic leap" forward for peace in the Middle East.
Leavitt said she did not know of any calls between Trump and Netanyahu following the announcement of the Saudi agreement.
There was no immediate reaction from Riyadh.
Should it go through, the deal to establish a civilian nuclear programme in Saudi Arabia would generate billions of dollars for American companies.
But with Trump's latest announcement, it was unclear whether it would ever see the light of day.
If Washington "tries to force normalisation on Riyadh, it's not likely to work out very well," said H.A. Hellyer, senior associate fellow at the Royal United Services Institute (RUSI), a leading UK defence and security think tank.
"Would Saudi decide to do a complete 180 on what it has declared for the past three years, or 30 years? Quite dubious," he told AFP.
Critics have also insisted that the deal could precipitate a nuclear race in a region already battered by war.
One provision would potentially see US companies build a uranium enrichment facility in Saudi Arabia, The Wall Street Journal reported.
The US Energy Department made no mention of such a provision and did not offer comment when asked about it by AFP.
Trump seemingly quashed it by saying on Truth Social that there would be "no enrichment of material!"
The deal will be submitted to Congress for review.
US lawmakers from both parties and Israeli officials have previously voiced opposition to such a project over fears Saudi Arabia could eventually convert it to develop nuclear weapons.
Secretary of State Marco Rubio said that any civil nuclear agreement signed by Washington with another country "is going to be one that will have safeguards in place to ensure that it can't be turned into a weapons programme."
PROLIFERATION CONCERNS
Riyadh, like Teheran, has long been adamant about its right to pursue a civilian nuclear programme. Last year it signed a mutual defence pact with nuclear-armed Pakistan.
Policymakers have long feared that if Iran produced a nuclear weapon, the move would set off an arms race across the Gulf.
US Energy Secretary Chris Wright dismissed concerns, insisting the deal was in Washington's economic and strategic interest and involved the "highest standards of nuclear safety and nonproliferation."
The United States has agreements governing nuclear cooperation with more than 50 countries.
The Saudi deal meanwhile comes as Riyadh looks set to be dragged further into the Middle East war, with Yemen's Houthi rebels starting to attack ships in the Red Sea to enforce what they say is a blockade on Saudi Arabia.
Trump vowed to punish both Iran and the Houthis if the attacks continue.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: World Bank confirms plan to phase out China lending by 2031
Article:
WASHINGTON: The World Bank on Thursday confirmed that it plans to phase out its lending to China by 2031, according to the organisation's new country partnership framework (CPF) for the world's second-largest economy.
AFP first reported that the multilateral lender would be making the move last month.
"IBRD lending will continue to phase down during the CPF period, not exceeding US$2 billion," the World Bank Group (WBG) said in a post on its website, referring to lending through the International Bank for Reconstruction and Development.
A CPF is a mutually agreed plan between the bank and a client country outlining development priorities for a multi-year period.
"In principle, no further borrowing is expected from IBRD by the end of the CPF period."
The WBG said the five-year CPF "marks a new phase in a 45-year partnership", as China moves from needing financing to primarily requiring technical assistance and knowledge sharing.
"As our partnership evolves, we are increasingly focused on knowledge, innovation and shared solutions," said Anna Bjerde, World Bank managing director of operations.
World Bank lending to China has steadily declined in recent years as the Asian giant saw explosive growth and a reduction in poverty indicators.
"As China tackles the challenges of an ageing society, a shifting economy, and other development priorities, we will work alongside it to generate ideas that matter not just for China, but for emerging markets around the world," said Bjerde.
China's Deputy Finance Minister Liao Min said China would continue to deepen engagement with the World Bank, regardless of the changing relationship with respect to lending.
The World Bank's new five-year plan agreed with China focuses on delivering economic growth while also providing better jobs, social resilience and a low-carbon economy.
In his first term in office, US President Donald Trump demanded that the World Bank stop lending to China entirely, as he adopted a more aggressive approach to Washington's chief economic rival.
Trump has maintained that tone in his second term, but has not specifically repeated that demand.
World Bank lending to China peaked at US$2.42 billion in 2017, but had fallen to US$750 million by 2025.
China also contributes funds to the World Bank's International Development Association (IDA) pool for the world's least developed countries, with its US$1.5 billion contribution under the latest replenishment round making Beijing the fifth-largest donor.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump won't block Tate brothers' extradition: White House
Article:
WASHINGTON: US President Donald Trump will not stop the extradition of influencer Andrew Tate and his brother Tristan to Britain on sex crime charges, the White House said on Thursday.
Self-professed misogynist Tate and his younger sibling were detained by US authorities in Miami on Saturday in a move their lawyer called "politically motivated".
The Tates are challenging their extradition.
"No," White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt told a news briefing when asked if Trump or the administration planned to intervene in the Tates' extradition process.
Secretary of State Marco Rubio has also distanced the administration from the case.
"There's no role for us to play at this moment or maybe ever in that regard," he told reporters in the Philippines on Wednesday.
The Tate brothers are avid supporters of Trump and were part of the hypermasculine "manosphere" that contributed to the Republican's 2024 US presidential election victory.
Andrew Tate also tried to cultivate relationships in the past with Trump's sons Don Jr and Barron, US media reported.
The Tates were arrested after British prosecutors announced new charges of rape, sex trafficking and assault. They now face 59 charges in total — 42 against Andrew and 17 against Tristan.
Their lawyer said on Monday that they were innocent and would fight extradition.
[[nid:1495953]]
"It's politically motivated. There's no question about it," lawyer Joseph McBride told reporters outside a Miami court after a hearing on the case.
In Romania, where they have been based in recent years, the Tates face allegations of trafficking minors, sexual intercourse with a minor and money laundering.
In March 2025, the US state of Florida launched a criminal investigation into the brothers. The status of that investigation is unclear.
Congresswoman Yassamin Ansari, a Democrat from Arizona, called for a probe into whether Trump administration officials, congressional offices or people close to the president improperly intervened to protect the Tates.
"No individual — regardless of wealth, political party, or access to the first family and the president — is above the law or beyond the reach of justice," she wrote in a letter to House Oversight Chairman James Comer.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Salman Rushdie testifies at knife attacker's US terror trial
Article:
NEW YORK: Novelist Salman Rushdie on Thursday testified at the federal trial of the man who tried to kill him with a knife at a New York cultural centre in 2022.
Hadi Matar, a 28-year-old Lebanese-American, was sentenced last year to 25 years in prison by New York state's justice system for attempted murder, which left Rushdie blind in one eye.
He is now standing trial on terrorism charges before a federal court in Buffalo, in northwestern New York.
Prosecutors allege that Matar was motivated by a fatwa that Ayatollah Khomeini, then leader of Iran, issued in 1989 following Rushdie's depiction of a fictionalised Prophet Muhammad in his novel The Satanic Verses.
They also accuse Matar of supporting Hizbollah, the pro-Iranian Lebanese movement that the United States considers a terrorist group, as investigators found materials from the organisation at his home.
Asked on Thursday if he believed Matar was motivated to carry out the fatwa, Rushdie replied: "I had no knowledge of that. I have my suspicions," according to US media.
Rushdie lived in seclusion in London for a decade after the 1989 fatwa, but for the past 20 years — until the attack — he had lived relatively normally in New York.
He spoke in court for nearly an hour about the assault in which Matar stabbed him about 10 times with a six-inch blade as Rushdie was about to address a free speech conference.
Matar's defence lawyers said he never acted on behalf of any terrorist organisation, even if he was angry about the blasphemous nature of The Satanic Verses.
If convicted, he faces a possible sentence of life in prison.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Six candidates to lead UN take stage for televised debate
Article:
UNITED NATIONS: The six candidates to lead the United Nations on Thursday convened to offer their visions for the world body, one week before the formal start of a selection process crucial to an organisation in deep crisis.
With the campaign struggling to generate real enthusiasm, the hopefuls faced 90 minutes of questions from diplomats, UN employees and civil society representatives at an event in the huge General Assembly Hall, broadcast by Bloomberg.
Chile's Michelle Bachelet, Ecuador's Maria Fernanda Espinosa, Rafael Grossi of Argentina, Costa Rica's Rebeca Grynspan, Guyana's Carolyn Rodrigues-Birkett and Macky Sall of Senegal are all in the running to succeed outgoing Secretary-General Antonio Guterres.
The first question came from one of the two moderators of the debate: "What makes you the best person to be secretary-general?"
Each candidate was given two minutes to respond.
"This UN town hall will... provide a rare opportunity to engage with the ideas, priorities and leadership vision of the candidates for one of the world's most important positions," General Assembly President Annalena Baerbock said.
"In an increasingly fragmented world, the United Nations needs not only an exceptional chief administrator who will make the organisation fit for the 21st century but also the leadership and integrity of someone willing to vigorously defend the UN Charter and all its three pillars," she added.
The United Nations is facing a profound financial and confidence crisis, with US President Donald Trump's administration accusing it of spending recklessly, spreading itself too thin and failing to deliver satisfactory results.
The United States is therefore demanding a return to the organisation's fundamental purposes of peace and security, while other states stress the equal importance of its other two pillars: human rights and development.
NO MISSTEPS
Against this backdrop, "the race does seem short on energy" compared with the livelier 2016 campaign, which had "more candidates and lots of intrigue" before Guterres was ultimately selected, International Crisis Group analyst Richard Gowan told AFP.
"This time around, everyone agrees that the race is incredibly important for the future of the UN, but the contest itself has been quite cautious," he added.
"There is a sense that the candidates are treading carefully to avoid offending the US and other veto powers."
The candidates have already faced three hours of questioning each from representatives of the 193 UN member states.
Most stressed the need to continue the organisation's ongoing reforms to make it more efficient, as well as the importance of greater involvement by the secretary-general in peace efforts around the world.
On July 30, the 15 members of the Security Council are due to hold their first closed-door, anonymous vote on their preferences among the candidates.
That first "straw poll" will be followed by an unspecified number of similar votes until one candidate can secure the required nine votes without a veto from any of the five permanent members — the United States, Britain, China, France and Russia.
The name of the selected candidate will then be sent to the General Assembly for the formal appointment to the position.
He or she will take office on Jan 1, 2027.
Several diplomats interviewed by AFP were cautious about the outcome of the race, saying a number of capitals had yet to make their choice.
"I find that Grossi is still the most frequently mentioned front-runner, but he has not locked the race up. Other candidates like Grynspan and Rodrigues-Birkett also have significant numbers of admirers," Gowan said, noting that a dark horse candidate could still emerge.
Many names are still circulating in UN corridors and the media, notably that of FIFA president Gianni Infantino, whom Trump would reportedly like to see take the helm of the UN, according to the New York Post.
Under a tradition of geographic rotation that is not always followed, Latin America is making a claim to the post this time. Sall is the only candidate not from the region.
Many states are also calling for a woman to hold the position for the first time.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Extreme floods to test Malaysia into next year
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
KUALA LUMPUR: Experts have warned that Malaysia will face extreme El Nino heat and severe flash floods that will continue until next year.
Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia climatologist Emeritus Professor Dr Fredolin Tangang said a strong El Nino would impact the nation from April this year to May next year.
He said the intensity of storms in Kuala Lumpur had risen steadily over the past 30 years.
Inadequate drainage and overdevelopment continued to worsen flooding, he added.
Between January and May, major storms struck Sabah, Sarawak and Selangor.
Storms in April and May also submerged key Klang Valley routes in Petaling Jaya, Ampang, Shah Alam, Sungai Besi, Gombak and Jalan Kuchai Lama, as well as the Bukit Jalil LRT station and Kesas Highway.
Universiti Malaya meteorologist Emeritus  Professor Datuk Dr Azizan Abu Samah cited an 82 per cent probability of El Nino between May and July, rising to 96 per cent from December to February.
He warned that a 10 to 20 per cent drop in rainfall could increase the risk of heatwaves and haze in September, before heavier rains return during the October-November inter-monsoon and northeast monsoon seasons.
To address the issue, Kuala Lumpur City Hall's 2026 budget allocates RM84.6 million for flood mitigation works, including RM45.15 million for development and RM39.45 million for operating costs, while RM32.18 million is set aside for slope maintenance and management.
In Selangor, the federal government, through the Energy Transition and Water Transformation Ministry, has handed over six completed projects worth RM83.46 million.
The projects comprise three coastal erosion control initiatives costing RM9.26 million in Sabak Bernam and Kuala Selangor, the RM60.5 million Morib Beach Coastal Conservation Project, a RM4.3 million riverbank stabilisation along a 1.2km stretch of Sungai Penchala and a RM9.4 million upgrade along 1.6km of Sungai Kemensah.
The government has also approved 25 projects with a total cost of RM3.639 billion.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Sabah steps up targeted efforts to curb rising croc attacks
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
KOTA KINABALU: Sabah is combining strategies to tackle rising human-crocodile conflict.
State Assistant Tourism, Culture and Environment Minister Dr Andi Md Shamsureezal Mohd Saina said its integrated approach was tailored to Sabah's challenging landscape, which spans vast river systems, coastlines and widespread conflict hotspots.
On July 18, the body of a 57-year-old man was recovered after he was attacked by a crocodile while cleaning a boat in Kampung Batu Putih. His 19-year-old son witnessed the attack.
Earlier in April, a 5-year-old girl went missing after she was reportedly snatched by a crocodile on a beach near Jalan Felda Sahabat 16, Desa Kencana.
Andi said that since 2023, the state government has strengthened safety efforts by establishing the Sabah Crocodile Mitigation Operations Committee, chaired by the State Affairs Office secretary.
"The committee brings together the Sabah Wildlife Department, police and other relevant agencies to coordinate operational responses, information-sharing and rapid action in high-risk conflict areas.
"Measures implemented include joint operations, managing high-risk crocodiles based on wildlife department assessments, installing warning signs, running public awareness campaigns and working with experts to reduce conflicts.
"Sabah's approach is based on the location and specific levels of conflict risk identified, rather than permanently stationing task forces along rivers," he told the state assembly here yesterday.
Andi was responding to Datuk Wong Hong Jun (Warisan-Tanjung Aru), who asked about the state government's plan in addressing human-crocodile conflict and whether Sabah would follow Sarawak's lead in setting up dedicated river task forces to capture crocodiles.
He added that under the 13th Malaysia Plan, the state would go on to tackle broader human-wildlife conflicts involving elephants, monkeys and other animals.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Maybank signs deal to boost Asean currency capital markets
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
KUALA LUMPUR: Malayan Banking Bhd (Maybank) and the Credit Guarantee and Investment Facility (CGIF), a trust fund of the Asian Development Bank (ADB), have signed a memorandum of understanding (MoU) to strengthen local currency capital markets across the Asean+3 region.
The MoU was signed during the inaugural joint forum organised by Maybank and CGIF, entitled "Accelerating Digital Infrastructure Investment Through the Bonds and Sukuk Markets 2026 — Securing Malaysia's Digital Future".
Maybank Group chief executive officer of global banking Datuk John Chong said the collaboration is built on a strong track record between the two organisations, which have previously worked together on bond and sukuk issuances in Malaysia, Vietnam, Thailand and Hong Kong.
"With foreign direct investment into Asia continuing to accelerate, we see significant opportunities to develop innovative financing solutions, deepen local capital markets and enable businesses to access more diverse funding sources to support their growth," he said.
Reflecting the positive outlook, Maybank has raised its forecast for Malaysia's private debt securities issuance this year to between RM160 billion and RM170 billion, up from its earlier estimate of RM150 billion.
The bank said the revised projection is supported by a strong issuance pipeline, resilient macroeconomic fundamentals and favourable domestic funding conditions.
It added that credit conditions are expected to remain stable in the second half of the year, underpinned by healthy economic fundamentals and continued strength in domestic capital markets.
CGIF chief executive officer Noriko Nasu said deep and resilient local currency bond markets are essential for financing Asia's long-term development priorities.
"Our partnership with Maybank brings together complementary capabilities to broaden access to capital, catalyse private sector investment and advance innovative financing solutions for the Asean+3 region," Noriko said.
The forum, held in collaboration with the Finance Ministry and Securities Commission (SC), brought together about 150 policymakers, corporate leaders and industry participants.
It featured a keynote address by SC executive director of corporate finance and investments Datuk Zain Azhari Mazlan and a fireside chat with Bank Negara Malaysia Deputy Governor Datuk Marzunisham Omar.
Both parties will also work to mobilise financing for energy transition, digital economy development and digital infrastructure projects, as well as to support issuers in accessing diversified funding sources.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: FBI, Cambodia vow cyber scam crackdown
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
PHNOM PENH: The United States' Federal Bureau of Investigation director Kash Patel has pledged stronger cooperation with Cambodia to dismantle cyber scam syndicates as online fraud inflicts billions of dollars in losses across Southeast Asia.
The cooperation emerged during his talks with Cambodian Prime Minister Hun Manet at the Peace Palace in Phnom Penh yesterday, online portal Fresh News reported.
Cambodia and the US are seeking to adopt a new approach in combating transnational crime, drug trafficking and online scams that have been threatening Southeast Asia.
Patel's visit comes days after the United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime  (UNODC) warned that combined annual losses from scam offences across East Asia, Southeast Asia, Australia and New Zealand were estimated at between US$88 billion and US$114 billion last year.
The combined annual retail value of the methamphetamine, ketamine and heroin markets was estimated at between US$75 billion and US$110 billion.
The organisation, in its report released earlier this week, said criminal syndicates had transformed into corporate-style networks, posing a challenge to governance, economic stability and development.
