"What we are seeing is a shift in which groups that stayed within their own geographic domain and criminal speciality are now operating across multiple illicit markets at once, relying on the same service streams," said Delphine Schantz, UNODC regional representative for Southeast Asia and the Pacific, in a press statement issued on Tuesday.
"Their operating model looks like corporate franchising — imagine specialised departments for laundering money, trafficking people, smuggling migrants and harvesting data, all plugged into the same, service-based, interconnected network."
The report, titled "An Interconnected Ecosystem: Transnational Organised Crime Threat Assessment for Southeast Asia July 2026", was released on Tuesday.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: NGOs to protest US pressure on Malaysia's policies
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Three non-governmental organisations (NGOs) will hold a peaceful assembly and submit a protest memorandum to the United States (US) Embassy tomorrow, objecting to what they described as attempts by US lawmakers to pressure Malaysia over its sovereign policies.
The organisations involved are the Malaysian Consultative Council of Islamic Organisations (Mapim), Muslim Youth Movement of Malaysia (Abim) and Pertubuhan Kebajikan Rakyat Sejagat Malaysia (Sejagat).
According to the organisers' programme poster, the gathering will begin at Tabung Haji Mosque at 3pm after Friday prayers, before participants proceed to the embassy.
Themed "Rakyat Bersatu Tolak Campur Tangan AS" (The People United Against US Interference), the gathering carries the slogan "Pertahankan Kedaulatan Negara" (Defend National Sovereignty).
Participants are expected to protest what organisers described as US political interference, while asserting Malaysia's right to determine its own foreign and entry policies.
Organisers have urged participants to follow security instructions and ensure the assembly is conducted peacefully, in unity and with discipline.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: At 102, Negri Sembilan's oldest government pensioner still says 'thank you'
Article:
SEREMBAN: "Thank you very much, thank you everyone."
Those were the heartfelt words of 102-year-old Ong Siew Choon, Negri Sembilan's oldest government pensioner, as she received a visit from Public Service Department (PSD) director-general Tan Sri Wan Ahmad Dahlan Abdul Aziz today.
Despite having difficulties with speech, hearing and vision, Siew Choon repeatedly expressed her gratitude throughout the visit.
Siew Choon served as a general worker and school cleaner under the Education Ministry from 1965 until her retirement in 1979.
Her daughter, Yeoh Kin Yeok, 75, said her mother remained generally healthy despite her age, although she now experienced hearing, vision and speech difficulties and was living with dementia.
"My father worked as a factory clerk, but as he grew older, he became ill and was unable to continue working before he died in 1972.
"My mother then became the family's sole breadwinner. She first worked as a domestic helper before becoming a school cleaner at the age of 41," she said at the family's home.
Kin Yeok, the third of six siblings and a retired teacher, said her mother's longevity was likely due to her vegetable-based diet and passion for gardening.
"I think her diet has played the biggest role. These days, though, we secretly encourage her to eat more protein to give her strength.
"She loved tending to her flower garden until about five years ago, when we asked her to stop because we were worried she might fall. Otherwise, she would spend every morning from 7.30am to 10am looking after her plants," she said.
She added that the family had not expected the visit from Wan Ahmad Dahlan and felt deeply honoured by the recognition.
Throughout the visit, Siew Choon continued thanking everyone present, touching those in attendance.
Meanwhile, Wan Ahmad Dahlan said the visit was part of the PSD's Program Ziarah Pesara, which saw seven government pensioners in the Seremban area receive visits in collaboration with the Retirement Fund (Incorporated) (KWAP) and the Negri Sembilan Implementation Coordination Unit (ICU).
He said the programme reflected the department's continued commitment to recognising the contributions and sacrifices of former civil servants while ensuring their welfare remained a priority after retirement.
During the visit, Wan Ahmad Dahlan presented Siew Choon with a new pensioner card, daily necessities and a contribution from the prime minister.
He said PSD, through its strategic partner KWAP, continued to maintain records of retired civil servants, enabling the department to trace and assist them even many years after retirement.
"All the data is with us. The challenge is finding the time and opportunity to visit them. Whenever we travel on official duty, I or PSD officers try to make time to meet retired civil servants.
"At the very least, we can ask about their wellbeing and provide assistance where needed, especially on health-related matters, including support with medical equipment," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Johor PH wants answers on 10 new deputy posts
Article:
JOHOR BARU: Eight Johor Pakatan Harapan (PH) assemblymen have called on the state government to explain its decision to appoint 10 deputy committee chairmen, saying the move should be guided by good governance rather than political considerations.
In a joint statement today, the lawmakers — Dr Maszlee Malik (Puteri Wangsa), Felicia Poh Rui Ling (Penggaram), Andrew Chen (Stulang), Ng Yak Howe (Bentayan), Wong Bor Yang (Senai), Chu Poh Yee (Mengkibol), Kartiyaini Jeyapalan (Skudai) and Nazri Abdul Rahman (Simpang Jeram) — said all administrative decisions should be based on the principles of necessity, effectiveness and accountability.
"The first issue is whether there is a real need for these 10 new posts. The Johor government has so far operated under a clear administrative structure, with support from professional civil servants, implementing agencies and special officers overseeing their respective portfolios.
"If there are indeed capacity constraints that require an additional layer in the administrative structure, what empirical evidence supports such a claim?" the statement said.
The assemblymen also questioned whether the appointments could lead to overlapping responsibilities, increased bureaucracy and additional costs, including allowances, administrative facilities, support staff and other operating expenses.
They urged the Johor government to publicly explain the rationale behind creating the posts, their roles and powers, the financial implications, and the key performance indicators that would be used to assess their effectiveness.
The lawmakers said if the appointments were intended to develop future leaders or ease the workload of existing officeholders, the objectives should be clearly articulated together with appropriate accountability mechanisms.
"As representatives of the people, our role is not just to criticise, but to ensure government decisions are guided by good governance, safeguard public funds and deliver real value to Johor's development.
"Johor needs a more efficient administration, rather than simply a larger one," the statement said.
Yesterday, Johor Menteri Besar Datuk Onn Hafiz Ghazi announced the appointments, saying they were aimed at strengthening the administration and improving public service delivery.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd
